coredata.dc:title: Carbon Dots as smoke suppression agents for the reduction of CO release in combustion and improvement of UV resistance towards Phosphorus-containing polyester
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Weiwen
Wei, Lifei
Ma, Tianyi
Wu, Yuhang
Zhang, Anying
Wei, Jianfei
Wang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: 2-Carboxyethyl(phenyl) phosphinic acid (CEPPA) is an effective phosphorus-containing flame retardant for PET. However, the release of poisonous and harmful smoke increases when CEPPA is added to PET matrix (CEPPA-PET) during the combustion process. To address this, biomass gelatin-based Carbon Dots (gCDs) was adopted as nontoxic green smoke suppression agent. When the gCDs was copolymerized into CEPPA-PET matrix by in situ polymerization (CEPPA-gCDs-PET), the peak smoke production rate was reduced by 59.38% and the CO yield was decreased by 13.83%. It was found the limited oxygen index of CEPPA-gCDs-PET rose to 31% from 21% and could achieve a V-0 rating in the UL-94 test when the content of gCDs and CEPPA were only 1.0 wt% and 5.0 wt%, respectively. What is more, in comparison with neat PET, the peak heat release rate significantly decreased by 42.66% and the total heat release was reduced by 48.10%, the peak smoke production rate is also reduced by 59.38%. Besides, the tensile strength of CEPPA-gCDs-PET increased by 11.93% for CEPPA-PET. At the same time, the addition of gCDs was beneficial for the improvement of UV resistance for CEPPA-PET, and the transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is less than 5% from 240 nm to 400 nm. Thus, a higher efficiency flame retardant, mechanical enhancement and UV resistance system was constructed with only 5 wt% CEPPA and 1 wt% gCDs toward CEPPA-PET copolymer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Smoke suppression agent
CEPPA
UV resistance
Polyester

head.ce:title: Carbon Dots as smoke suppression agents for the reduction of CO release in combustion and improvement of UV resistance towards Phosphorus-containing polyester

Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile Nano Fiber

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, No. 399 BinShuiXi Road, Xiqing District, Tianjin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2-Carboxyethyl(phenyl) phosphinic acid (CEPPA) is an effective phosphorus-containing flame retardant for PET. However, the release of poisonous and harmful smoke increases when CEPPA is added to PET matrix (CEPPA-PET) during the combustion process. To address this, biomass gelatin-based Carbon Dots (gCDs) was adopted as nontoxic green smoke suppression agent. When the gCDs was copolymerized into CEPPA-PET matrix by in situ polymerization (CEPPA-gCDs-PET), the peak smoke production rate was reduced by 59.38% and the CO yield was decreased by 13.83%. It was found the limited oxygen index of CEPPA-gCDs-PET rose to 31% from 21% and could achieve a V-0 rating in the UL-94 test when the content of gCDs and CEPPA were only 1.0 wt% and 5.0 wt%, respectively. What is more, in comparison with neat PET, the peak heat release rate significantly decreased by 42.66% and the total heat release was reduced by 48.10%, the peak smoke production rate is also reduced by 59.38%. Besides, the tensile strength of CEPPA-gCDs-PET increased by 11.93% for CEPPA-PET. At the same time, the addition of gCDs was beneficial for the improvement of UV resistance for CEPPA-PET, and the transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is less than 5% from 240 nm to 400 nm. Thus, a higher efficiency flame retardant, mechanical enhancement and UV resistance system was constructed with only 5 wt% CEPPA and 1 wt% gCDs toward CEPPA-PET copolymer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is widely used as fiber [1], film [2], fabric [3] and engineering plastics [4,5] in production and daily life due to its excellent mechanical properties, thermal degradation stability and chemical resistance. However, the limited oxygen index (LOI) of PET is only 21% and it could release a lot of smoke and toxic gases during the burning process [6]. Therefore, the development of flame-retardant and smoke-suppressing polyester is an urgent problem that needs to be solved.

Phosphorus flame retardants are widely used for low toxicity and high efficiency [7], such as APP [8], DOPO [9]and PDPSI [10] et al. However, It was found that the free radical inhibitors produced by phosphorus-containing flame retardants in the gas phase interrupt the complete combustion of PET fragments, thus producing more non-combustible smoke particles and CO [11,12]. However, CO is the main cause of people suffocation to death in fire. When phosphorus-containing flame retardants and carbon materials act cooperatively with the PET matrix, carbon material can better reduce matrix smoke release and COP due to promoting char formation during the material combustion process. Lifei Wei et al. [12] prepared a noval flame retardant (GO-HCCP) by nucleophilic substitution of GO, which was applied in PET by in situ polymerization technique. In comparation with PET, the COP was reduced by 37.5% and pSPR decreased to 0.20 m2/s from 0.24 m2/s. Yinghao Song et al. [13] reported that a new type of three-in-one flame retardant (CA) was constructed for PET with ammonium polyphosphate (APP) as shell material and carbon microspheres (CMSs) as core. The research results show that the COP was reduced by 66.7% and TSP decreased to 13.6 m2 from 14.4 m2.

Carbon Dots is a kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm, which is receiving considerable attention thanks to the biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost-effective sources. Our previous study found that the gCDs could promote the formation of an effective barrier and gCDs is a perfect smoke suppression agent for PET [14]. Besides, the tensile strength of PET increase under the influence of gCDs. It is worth noting that the gCDs also has excellent UV resistance [15].

In this work, the copolymerized flame retardant CEPPA was used, but the higher addition of CEPPA often affects the mechanical properties of PET. Based on that, a higher efficiency flame retardant, mechanical enhancement and UV resistance system was constructed with only 5 wt% CEPPA and 1 wt% gCDs. Two reference copolymers, PET containing CEPPA (CEPPA-PET) and PET containing gCDs (gCDs-PET) were also prepared comparably.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: A biomass material gelatin (GA, analytically pure) was purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol (EG, industrial grade) and Terephthalic acid (TPA, industrial grade) was received from Sinopec Group (Tianjin, China). A hydrolytic agent HyMax 1010 (1010, industrial grade) was obtained from Shanghai Langyi Functional Materials Co., Ltd. Triphenyl phosphite (TPI, analytically pure) and Diantimony trioxide (Sb2O3, analytically pure) with analysis grade was offered by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 2-carboxyethyl(phenyl) phosphoric acid esterifying liquid with EG (CEPPA-EG, industrial grade) was obtained from Beijing Yanshan Petrochemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymerization of flame retardant PET
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous study, gCDs was prepared by the hydrothermal process with gelatin precursors [14]. TPA, gCDs, CEPPA-EG, EG and Sb2O3 mixture in a specific ratio were placed in the polymerization reactor referred to Table 1 . CEPPA-EG and gCDs were prepared before the experiment. The mixture was dispersed by an impeller for 30 min. Then, with continuous stirring, the temperature was raised to 240 °C, and the reaction was allowed to proceed at 320 kPa for about 2 h. This process was defined as high-pressure esterification. Whether the esterification reaction was completed entirely depends on the amount of water removed from the esterification reaction. After that, the pressure was released slowly, which lasted for 1 h. Subsequently, HyMax 1010 (0.7 g), TPI (0.7 g), and EG (15 g) were added to the polymerization reactor for 0.5 h in a standard atmosphere. This process was defined as atmospheric pressure esterification. After that, the reaction temperature was released to 270 °C and maintained for 3 h under vacuum conditions. This product was defined as flame retardant PET copolymer (Fig. 1 ).Table 2. .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization
ce:section.ce:para:
The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was recorded on the Nicolet spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm-1and scanning 64 times. The 1H NMR data of PET copolymer was provided on the Bruker NMR (Bruker 500, USA) at room temperature and the solvent is trifluoroacetic acid (TFA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was obtained with an Thermo ESCALAB (250Xi, USA). The glass transition behavior of flame retardant polyester was investigated by employed on a DSC Q2000 (TA, USA) with a rate of 10 °C/min, heating from 30 to 280 °C and cooling from 280 to 30 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The degree of crystallinity was calculated using the following equation:
X c
=
Δ
H c
( 1 -
W f
/ 100 )
Δ
H m
× 100
, where Xc is the degree of crystallinity; ΔHm is the melting enthalpy of 100% crystalline PET, which is 140 J/g [16]; ΔHc is the melting enthalpy of PET copolymer and Wf is the mass fraction of the flame retardants in the copolymer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a thermo-analyzer (TG 209F1, GER) under air and nitrogen atmospheres with a flow rate of 10 °C/min from room temperature to 700 °C. According to ASTM D2863-2008, the limiting oxygen index test (LOI) was obtained with a Dynisco oxygen index meter (DYNISCO, USA). The dimensions of the sample were 100.0 × 6.5 × 3.0 mm3. Each sample was conducted five times. The vertical burning (UL-94) test according to ASTM D3801 was carried out on the horizontal-vertical burning tester (CZF-3, China), and the dimensions of the samples were 130.0 × 13.0 × 3.0 mm3. The fire behavior of flame retardant polyesters was characterized with a cone calorimeter test (FTTL, UK) based on ISO 5660–1 at an external heat flux of 50 kW/m2. The dimensions of the samples were 100.0 × 100.0 × 3.0 mm3 and the cone calorimeter test for each sample was conducted three times. The fractured char-layer surface was analyzed using a JSM-7500F (JEOL, Japan) scanning electron microscope (SEM) at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. Raman spectrum of char residue in cone calorimeter test was taken with a LABRAM-HR laser confocal Raman microscope (Renishaw,UK) spectrometer equipped with a 325 nm laser source. The evolved gaseous decomposition products were carried on a thermogravimetric-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR), using a TGA thermogravimetric analyzer (TA5500, USA) under nitrogen and air atmosphere coupled with a Nicolet FTIR spectrophotometer (iS50, USA). Firstly, the sample was placed in an EGA/PY-3030 pyrolyzer (Frontier, Japan) under a nitrogen atmosphere by heating from ambient to 435 °C and then pyrolyzed for 300 s. Afterwards, the products were carried through helium to the DB-1MS capillary column wherein the inlet temperature of the capillary column (30.0 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) is 300 °C. Besides, the MS indicator was operated in the electron impact mode at the electron energy of 70 eV and the scanning range of 20–650 m/z.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Structure characterization of PET and PET copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, PET and PET copolymer are prepared and their structure is characterized by 1H NMR and XPS spectroscopy.

The 1H NMR spectrum of PET and PET copolymer are shown in Fig. 2 . The 1H NMR spectrum of PET and PET copolymer are shown in Fig. 2. The chemical shifts of H protons of aromatic and aliphatic are between 7.5 and 8.4 and 3.9–4.9 ppm [17,18]. The peak at 8.1–8.3 ppm (1) is attributed to the H of the benzene ring of PET, while the chemical shift at 4.70 ppm (2) is ascribed to the methylene of the ester group. Besides, compared with the spectrum of pure PET, the additional peaks in the spectrum of the gCDs-PET are supposed to be related to gCDs. As shown in Fig. 2b that the R-NH2 is also explored at δ = 0.5 ppm. These results confirm that gCDs-PET is prepared successfully. The peak at 7.6–7.9 ppm (6) is reasonably assigned to the hydrogen proton of aryl of CEPPA and the peaks at 0.7 (4) and 0.8 ppm (3) are attributed to hydrogen proton on phosphoric acid and methylene of CEPPA, respectively, which indicates the CEPPA is copolymerized into PET chains. It is worth noting that the characteristic chemical shift of CEPPA-PET and gCDs-PET are also observed in CEPPA-gCDs-PET, which indicates that the CEPPA-gCDs-PET are prepared. Moreover, the chemical shift at 11.50 ppm is the characteristic peak of the solvent TFA, which had not been removed.

To further prove the successful preparation of CEPPA-gCDs-PET, XPS is adapted to detect the chemical composition of PET and PET copolymer. The survey XPS spectrum of CEPPA-gCDs-PET shows C, O, N and P elements (Fig. S1).

C1s (Fig. 3 a) spectra show three different bands where the binding energies are about 284.70 eV, 286.24 eV, and 288.66 eV, respectively. The band around 284.70 eV is contributed by the CC bonds of the benzene ring [19]. The appearance of the band at 286.24 eV is the contribution of the CO group [20]. The band centered at around 288.66 eV can be assigned to the CO group of ester-based [21]. The C1s peaks of the CEPPA-PET composite at 284.70 eV, 286.24 eV and 288.66 eV belong to CC/CP, COC/COP and CO. From Table S2, in comparison with COC/COP, the relative area radio of CO/C-PO is 0.64, which indicates that a mass of methylene exists in the CEPPA molecular chain contributed to CO is generated during the esterification process. Compared with neat PET, C1s of the spectrum of gCDs-PET at a binding energy of 287.58 eV, 284.70 eV, 286.24 eV, 288.66 eV correspond to OC-N, CC, CO, and CO, respectively [22]. At the same time, the area proportion of CO is lower than pure PET, which demonstrates that the ester group in the PET molecular chain is replaced by amide groups, resulting in the area proportion of CO reduction. In the XPS spectrum of CEPPA-gCDs-PET (Fig. 3g), the peaks correspond to CC/CP/CC (284.70 eV), OC (286.24 eV), CO (286.70 eV), COP (287.58 eV), OC-N (288.66 eV) and the area proportion is higher than other PET copolymer. These results provide evidences for successful functionalization of PET with gCDs and CEPPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of PET and PET copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: The glass transition behavior and thermal performance of PET and PET copolymer are investigated by DSC measurement [23]. The detailed information of PET and PET copolymer can be seen in the supporting information. The result (Fig. 4 and Table S3) indicates that CEPPA and gCDs are copolymerized into PET macromolecules, decreasing molecular chain uniformity and crystallinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis and flame retardancy of PET and PET copolymer
Thermogravimetric analysis of PET and PET copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal and thermal-oxidative stability of the samples is measured by TGA [24]. The thermogravimetric analysis of gCDs, CEPPA, and CEPPA-EG can be seen in the supporting information. As shown in Fig.S6, and Table S4.

PET and PET copolymer have only one thermal decomposition stage under nitrogen atmosphereFig 4. It can be found from Table 2 that the neat PET mass loss is 5 wt% at 400.50 °C (T5wt%), and the char residual is 10.57 wt% at 700 °C (C700°Cwt%). What is more, the T5wt% of CEPPA-PET decreases to 395.42 °C from 400.50 °C because the poor stability of PO of CEPPA than CC bond contributes to the degradation of CEPPA-PET during the burning process. The C700°Cwt% increases to 12.08% from 10.75% because the char layer covers the surface of PET and plays an isolation role, delaying the heating rate of the internal substrate. When the gCDs is copolymerized into PET chains, compared with neat PET, the T5wt% and maximum thermal decomposition temperature (Tmax) increase, which indicates that the thermal degradation stability of the PET matrix is improved under the influence of gCDs. Moreover, the mass of char residue for gCDs-PET rises to 14.75% from 10.57%, indicating that gCDs has the effect of promoting char formation [25].

As shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d that the weight loss of thermal degradation for PET and PET composite can be divided into two stages under the air atmosphere. The first stage of weight loss is caused by the flammable small molecules and combustible gases derived from the fracture of the PET copolymer chains between 300 and 490 °C. The second stage of weight loss under the temperature range from 490 to 600 °C is attributed to the further oxidation and degradation of macromolecules, which is not decomposed entirely in the previous stage [26]. Compared with neat PET, the T5wt% of CEPPA-gCDs-PET increased from 362.43 °C to 368.46 °C, which indicates gCDs inhabits the decomposition of CEPPA-PET. Besides, the C700°Cwt% of CEPPA-gCDs-PET increased to 3.80% from no residual char of PETTable 2. This result may be caused by the gCDs promoting the formation of char layers with barrier protection [14]. In the meantime, the phosphate ester in CEPPA underwent dehydration to form char, which can protect the inner matrix and delay degradation [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant performance of PET and PET copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the flame retardant properties of CEPPA and gCDs for PET substrate, LOI and UL-94 tests are used and the relevant data are listed in Table 3 . The process of UL-94 specific flame retardancy is shown in Fig. 5 .The pure PET is flammable and its value of LOI is only 21.2%. Compared with neat PET, the LOI value of CEPPA-PET increased to 29.7% from 21.2% and the UL-94 test increased to V-2 from no vertical rating. The reason can be attributed to the that CEPPA decomposes in advance, causing the break of PET macromolecule chains, which enhances the fluidity of PET melt and the phenomenon of melting droplets is presented during the burning process. When gCDs is added to PET, the LOI value of gCDs-PET copolymer increases to 26.3% and the UL-94 test is V-2. With the incorporation of gCDs into the CEPPA-PET matrix, the LOI value of CEPPA-gCDs-PET increases to 31.4% from 29.7%, while the UL-94 rate increase to V-0, which indicates that gCDs and CEPPA improve the flame retardant performance of PET synergistically.

The flame retardant performance of PET and PET copolymer is also evaluated by cone calorimetric test (CCT)[28]. The heat release rate (HRR), total heat release (THR), smoke production rate (SPR) and carbon monoxide production (COP) curves are presented in Fig. 6 and the corresponding data are summarized in Table 4 .

The time to ignition (TTI) is an important parameter to evaluate the fire resistance for polymer. TTI of CEPPA-PET copolymer increases to 57 s from 52 s for pure PET, which reason may be the phosphorus-containing segments decompose in advance, and the free radicals are released to capture flammable molecular fragments in the gas phase [12]. The TTI of gCDs-PET drops from 52 s for pure PET to 50 s, which is because gCDs absorbs heat first and transfer heat into the nearby molecular chain [14,29]. However, it is worth noting that the TTI for CEPPA-gCDs-PET copolymer is delayed by 10 s compared with CEPPA-PET copolymer. The fact demonstrated that CEPPA and gCDs have a synergistic effect on improving TTI of PET.

The HRR is the most critical parameter to predict the hazard of combustion. The higher HRR and peak heat release rate (PHRR), the faster the thermal degradation rate for polymer [30]. Fig. 6a depicts the HRR curves for neat PET and PET copolymer, and the results reveal that the pure PET burns fiercely after ignition and the PHRR is 1144 KW/m2. In a comparation with pure PET, the PHRR for CEPPA-PET decreased by 26.66%. The PHRR for gCDs-PET is 694 KW/m2 and the t-PHRR is earlier than 12 s pure PET, because the gCDs is copolymerized in PET macromolecules chains, which generate that the rate of heat transfer is accelerated [31,32]. Meanwhile, the PHRR of gCDs-PET decrease to 694 kW/m2 from 1144 kW/m2 for neat PET and the curves sharp of HRR become flattered, which indicates gCDs promotes the formation of the shield char layers and that impedes the heat transfer of the solid and gas phases. It is worth noting that the PHRR for CEPPA-gCDs-PET decreases 47.46% compared with neat PET which is probably caused by the generation of denser and more s char layers in the synergy of gCDs and CEPPA.

The HRR and THR can be combined to evaluate the combustion and flame retardant properties of materials more comprehensively. THR represents the total amount of heat released during the combustion process. Fig. 6b shows the THR curves of neat PET and PET copolymer, while the corresponding data are listed in Table 4. In comparison with neat PET, CEPPA-PET, gCDs-PET, and CEPPA-gCDs-PET decrease 39.24%, 11.39%, 48.10%, respectively. In terms of the CEPPA-gCDs-PET, the value of THR substantially decrease. The reason is that the free radical inhibitors of CEPPA in the gas phase interrupt the complete combustion of PET fragments and a residual protective char layer is formed under the action of gCDs.

Fig. 6c is the curves of SPR, and peak smoke production rate (PSPR) data are listed in Table 4. In comparison with pure PET, the curve of CEPPA-PET is above neat PET curve, which indicates that a mass of smoke particles is formed on the surface of the substrate. The reason is that early degradation of CEPPA causes the matrix to produce a large number of flammable molecular fragments, which are far beyond the controllable range of phosphorus-containing fragments [12]. When the gCDs is copolymerized into PET matrix, the shape of the SPR curve changes from sharp to flat, and the PSPR value of gCDs-PET is 0.13 m2/s, which is the lowest value among PET and PET copolymer. The reason is that gCDs promotes carbonization of PET matrix and forms an effect shielding layer to prevent the escape of flammable molecular fragments. When the gCDs is added to CEPPA-PET matrix, the PSPR reduces by 59.38% compared to CEPPA-PET, which indicates gCDs is beneficial to reduce the smoke production rate of CEPPA-PET.

Compared with PET, the CO2 yield of CEPPA-PET decreased by 21%, while the CO Yield of CEPPA-PET increased by 37.23%, which indicates that a large amount of flammable smoke fragment is not burned entirely due to radical inhibitor interrupting the combustion reaction under the influence of CEPPA. In terms of gCDs-PET, compared with neat PET, the CO2 yield and CO yield decreased by 9% and 16.95%, respectively. It is demonstrated that gCDs is favorable for thermal degradation and smoke particles of complete combustion [14]. It is worth noting that the CO yield of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is lower than CEPPA-PET copolymer 13.83% and the CO2 yield decrease by 13.93% because gCDs promotes thermal degradation and forms shielded char layers to prevent flammable gas from escaping at the end of combustion.

In addition to the above parameters intuitive. The degree of combustion hazard can also be evaluated by calculating the relationship between the parameters of flame retardants. FGI reflects the ability of materials to respond to heat and is defined as the radio of PHRR to t-PHRR [29]. FPI is defined as the ratio of TTI to PHRR and longer TTI or lower PHRR can reduce fire risk [33]. As shown in Table 5 , in comparison with pure PET, the FGI values of CEPPA-PET, gCDs-PET, CEPPA-gCDs-PET decrease by 33.92%, 41.69%, 50.44%, respectively, because gCDs and CEPPA extend the time for PET matrix to reach PHRR. In terms of FPI, the higher value represents the better flame retardancy. When the CEPPA and gCDs are added to the PET matrix at the same time, the values of FPI increase by 59.82%, which has a similar trend change to FGI.

FGI = PHRR/tPHRR FPI = TTI/PHRR.
(1)
E
Barrierandprotective
= 1 -
PHRR
FRPET
×
THR
PET

PET
×

E
Flameinhibition
= 1 -
EHC
FRPET
EHC
PET
(3)
E
charringeffect
= 1 -
TML
FRPET
TML
PET

To further study the specific flame-retardant mode, the results are quantified according to Eqs. (1)–(3) [33] and summarized in Table 5. The proportion of flame inhibition is estimated to be 30.12% for CEPPA-PET and with the combined action of CEPPA and gCDs, the value of E flame inbibition grew up to 41.09%. The result indicates that CEPPA plays a significant role in gas phase flame retardation. Meanwhile, gCDs acts in synergy with CEPPA in CEPPA-gCDs-PET copolymer. That fact can be caused by releasing free radicals catchers of CEPPA to interrupt the chain reaction in the gas phase, which delays the combustion of flammable fragments during the combustion process. At the same time, the free radical scavenging effect of gCDs inhibits the combustion of small molecular fragments.

When the CEPPA is added into PET copolymer, the Ebarrier and protective is −20.70% and the values of Echarring effect get to 20.18%. The result indicates that CEPPA promotes the formation of a mass of lower-quality char layers, which are difficult to shield flammable fragments from escaping to the substrate surface. When gCDs is added to the PET matrix, the Ebarrier and protective values of gCDs-PET get to 31.54% and the Echarring effect is 7.90%, which means that the gCDs promotes the formation of effective shielding char layers and play a barrier role in the flame retardant process. Similarly, a mass of denser and more solid char layers is generated under the influence of gCDs, when gCDs is added to the CEPPA-PET. Hence, gCDs improve CEPPA-PET shielding ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on flame retardant mechanism of PET and PET copolymer
Digital photographs and SEM images of the char residual after the CCT
ce:section.ce:para: The char residual of PET and PET copolymer after the CCT are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be found from Fig. 7a-7d that there are a lot of holes on the surface of pure PET char layers, and the inner and outer holes are interpenetrated with each other. The results indicate that the char layer of neat PET not only cannot effectively protect the internal substrate but also the generated heat and be quickly transferred to the inside of the substrate during the combustion process. In the meantime, the flammable fragments and oxygen can be smoothly diffused into the inside of the matrix, accelerating the combustion and decomposition of PET. When the CEPPA is added to the PET matrix, some large area holes on the char layer surface. The reason is that CEPPA releases a mass of free radical inhibitors and captures flammable PET fragments to volatilize to the substrate surface at high temperature. Besides, according to the CCT results the quality of residual char is low (Fig. 6h). For gCDs-PET, the inner char residue is continuous and complete (Fig. 7l). The fact can be attributed to the that gCDs can promote the formation of char layers and enhance the continuity of char layers in the condensed phase, and it corresponds to the effect of the char barrier on CCT results. Meanwhile, an expanded char layer is formed due to the effect of the char barrier (Fig. 7i). When the gCDs is added to the CEPPA-PET matrix, the SEM image of char residue for CEPPA-gCDs-PET (Fig. 7P) displays a continuous and dense char layer because the gCDs make up the defect of porous char layers for CEPPA-PET copolymer. As a result, gCDs and CEPPA is complementary in the formation of dense and continuous char layer, which is critical for flame retardant modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR of the residual char after the cone calorimeter test
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR is performed to study the structure of char residues (Fig. S4). The detailed information can be seen in the supporting information and the results indicate that the hydrogen is removed from the benzene ring, the char is burned more complete and the structure of residual char is more s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman of the char residual after the CCT
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectroscopy is conducted to characterize the degree of graphitization and neatness of the char layer [34]. Fig. 8 illustrates the Raman spectra of the char residues of PET and PET copolymer. The band D is located at 1360 cm−1, which belongs to amorphous char, while the G band at 1595 cm−1 represents the CC stretching vibration peak of ordered char [35]. Generally, the graphitization degree of the char residue can be expressed by the ratio of D band to G band comprehensive area (ID/IG) integrated areas, and the lower value of ID/IG demonstrates a higher graphitization degree for char layers [36]. At the same time, IG/IAll represents the ratio of graphitized carbon to total char.

The presence of CEPPA has a positive effect on the ID/IG. However, the IG/IAII of CEPPA-PET decreases to 19.47% from 24.05% of neat PET, which indicates that the CEPPA promotes the formation of lower quality char layers. When the gCDs is copolymerized into the PET matrix, the ID/IG value of PET decreases, suggesting the graphitized degree of residual char is improved. Meanwhile, the IG/IAll increased to 25.40% from 24.05% for PET. It is essential that the ID/IG of CEPPA-gCDs-PET decreases to 1.53 from 1.57 of the CEPPA-PET copolymer when the gCDs is copolymerized to CEPPA-PET, and the IG/IAII increases to 24.91% from 19.47%. Therefore, gCDs benefits char residue formation with a higher degree of graphitization. These results are also corresponding to cone test results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame mechanism of PET with the interaction between gCDs and CEPPA
ce:section.ce:para: It can be founded that the initial main pyrolysis products of PET and PET copolymer are 4-((2-((4-formylbenzoyl)oxy)ethoxy)carbonyl)benzoic acid (5), 2-((4-formylbenzoyl)oxy)ethyl methyl terephthalate (7), and 4-((2-((4-(methoxycarbonyl)benzoyl)oxy)ethoxy)carbonyl)benzoic acid (10) in step1 (Fig. 9). In terms of CEPPA-gCDs-PET, the 4-((2-((4-formylbenzoyl)oxy)ethoxy)carbonyl)benzoic acid content increase to 2.96% from 1.58% for neat PET, the 2-((4-formylbenzoyl)oxy)ethyl methyl terephthalate increase to 1.02% from 0.80%, and the 4-((2-((4-(methoxycarbonyl)benzoyl)oxy)ethoxy)carbonyl)benzoic acid increase 1.71% from 0% for pure PET. It is demonstrated that the degree of initial degradation decrease under the action of CEPPA and PET [12].

Major gas-phase products are benzoic acid (1) and 4-formylbenzoic acid (2) in the step3 stage (Fig. 9). In comparison with neat PET, the content of 4-formylbenzoic acid (2)for CEPPA-PET and gCDs-PET is higher than the content of benzoic acid(1), which indicates that CEPPA and gCDs accelerate the fracture of the CO connecting methylene. It's worth noting that benzoic acid is easier to form when the gCDs and CEPPA are added into the PET matrix together, which indicates that the decarboxylation reaction occurs to produce more CO2 under the combined action of gCDs and CEPPA. In step 4, the main degradation products in step 3 combine with various free radicals, including benzene free radical and aliphatic free radical to promotethe formation of benzene-containing macromolecules, including 2-(benzoyloxy)ethyl 4-formylbenzoate(3), Benzaldehyde(4), [1,1′-biphenyl]-4-carboxylic acidChemical (6), ethyl 4-formylbenzoate free radical (8), 4-ethylbenzoic acid (11), benzaldehyde(12) and so on.

To further investigate the combustion behavior and meteorological flame retardancy mechanism, the 3D TG-IR spectra are used to analyze the thermal degradation process [37,38]. The thermal interpretation gas-phase products of each sample are shown in Fig. 10 (a), Fig.S8 and Fig.S9. The FTIR of PET and PET copolymer volatilized decomposition products at 435 °C are presented in Fig. 10(a). It is noted that there are strong signals characteristic around 3580 (OH), 2750 (CO2), 2350 (aldehyde), 2190 (CO) and 1760, 1257, 730 cm−1 (benzoic acid). Hence, the main products of the thermal decomposition of PET are H2O, CO2, CO, –CHO and benzoic acid, etc., while the characteristic peak of benzoic acid of PET copolymer is vague.

the Gram-schmidt curves are closely related to the total pyrolysis product intensity [39]. From Fig. 10(b), it can be seen that the decomposition rate of PET is accelerated under the action of gCDs, while CEPPA does not accelerate the degradation rate of PET. When gCDs is added to CEPPA-PET, the thermal degradation rate increase. This result can illustrate that gCDs accelerates the degradation of CEPPA-PET.

According to the above discussion, the proposed flame retardant mechanism of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is proposed and presented in Fig. 11 . When the gCDs is added to CEPPA-PET matrix, the rate of thermal decomposition increases at the initial degradation. As the intensity of the burning increases, CEPPA releases PO· and PO2· to quench radical reactions in the gas phase, while the gCDs further accelerate CEPPA-gCDs-PET thermal degradation. At the middle and end of the combustion process, the CEPPA promotes the formation of a mass of low-quality and loose char-layers. Fortunately, the gCDs exerts a barrier effect and makes up for a porous defect in the CEPPA-gCDs-PET matrix. As a result, the joint action of gCDs and CEPPA promotes the formation of dense and continuous char layers, which is critical for flame retardant modification. Thus, a complementary flame retardant system toward PET based on the commercialized CEPPA is success constructed.

According to the above discussion, the proposed flame retardant mechanism of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is proposed and presented in Fig. 11. When the gCDs is added to CEPPA-PET matrix, the rate of thermal decomposition increases at the initial degradation. As the intensity of the burning increases, CEPPA releases PO· and PO2· to quench radical reactions in the gas phase, while the gCDs further accelerate CEPPA-gCDs-PET thermal degradation. At the middle and end of the combustion process, the CEPPA promotes the formation of a mass of low quality and loose char layers. Fortunately, the gCDs exerts a barrier effect and makes up for a porous defect in the CEPPA-gCDs-PET matrix. As a result, the joint action of gCDs and CEPPA promotes the formation of dense and continuous char layers, which is critical for flame retardant modification. Thus, a complementary flame retardant system toward PET based on the commercialized CEPPA is success constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanical properties of PET and PET copolymer
The spinnability and anti-UV property of PET and PET copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the possibility of CEPPA-gCDs-PET industrialization, adopting the melt spinning process to prepare the fiber. As shown in Fig. 13 (a) below, the golden fiber is prepared by melt spinning process. Compared with pure PET, the breaking strength of CEPPA-gCDs-PET fiber did not decrease significantly Table 8 .

As shown in Fig. 13(b), the sleeves are prepared from the fiber in the circular knitting machine and the anti-UV property are tested (Fig. 13(c)). Compared with sleeves prepared from the fiber of pure PET, the ultraviolet transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-PET increases significantly, which means CEPPA has adverse side effects on the anti-UV property of PET. However, the ultraviolet transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-gCDs-PET, which is even lower than that of sleeves prepared from the fiber of PET, decreases markedly under the gCDs action. It is worth noting that the transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is less than 5% from 240 nm to 400 nm. As shown in Fig.S10. It was found that there was an obvious absorption peak in UV region in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of gCDs due to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 [40]. What is more, As a fluorescent nanomaterial, gCDs can absorb ultraviolet light and emit blue light [41]. Based on the above analysis, the copolymerization of gCDs into CEPPA-PET molecular chains may confer CEPPA-PET with UV resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: To improve flame retardant performance and smoke suppression of PET, the traditional flame-retardant CEPPA can be adopted as a flame retardant to prepare flame retardant copolyesters. However, a mass of smoke is released and the char layers cannot form an effective shielding to inhibit the heat release. Besides, the anti-UV of PET decreases significantly. To solve this problem, gCDs is prepared to construct smoke suppression, UV resistance and mechanical enhancement multifunction flame retardant system in CEPPA-PET copolymer. The thermal performance and flame retardancy for PET and PET copolymer are studied and the synergistic flame retardant mechanism of gCDs and CEPPA in the PET substrate are discussed. The main content and conclusions of this research are as follows:
Compared with CEPPA-PET, the PSPR of CEPPA-gCDs-PET reduces by 59.38%, and the CO release yield decreases by 13.83%.

2. The LOI value rose to 31% from 21% and achieved a V-0 rating in the UL-94 test when only 1.0 wt% gCDs and 5.0 wt% CEPPA were added to the PET matrix. Compared with neat PET, the PHRR significantly decreased by 42.66% and the THR reduced by 48.10% for CEPPA-gCDs-PET.
3. gCDs promotes the formation of effective barrier char layers and make up the defect of CEPPA-PET in the char formation effect.
4. When the gCDs is copolymerized into the CEPPA-PET, the tensile strength increase by 11.93%.
5. gCDs endows CEPPA-PET with excellent UV resistance, and the transmittance of sleeves prepared from the fiber of CEPPA-gCDs-PET is less than 5% from 240 nm to 400 nm.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2222054), Beijing Scholars Program (RCQJ20303), Science Technology Plan of Beijing Municipal Education Commission (KM202110012007), and Special Fund for High-level Teacher Team Construction of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (BIFTXJ202225).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
